one_line_takeaway: A reference architecture and single qualitative proof-of-concept for an 'MCP Gateway' that offloads OAuth 2.1 auth, zero-trust tunnelling, and WAF/IDS from self-hosted MCP servers using composed open-source tools (Traefik, CrowdSec, Pangolin/WireGuard); useful as an engineering pattern but lacking any quantitative security or performance evaluation.
key_finding: Evaluation was explicitly QUALITATIVE, not quantitative. Qualitative testing showed the gateway enforced authentication (Traefik blocked unauthenticated requests, rejected invalid Authorization tokens), Traefik rate limiting plus CrowdSec blocked excessive requests, and backend servers were isolated via WireGuard. The author concludes that centralized authentication and WAF avoid per-server replication of security logic, and that off-the-shelf open-source components (Traefik, CrowdSec, Pangolin/WireGuard) can be composed into a spec-compliant (OAuth 2.1, MCP 2025-03-26) enterprise gateway. No latency, throughput, detection-rate, or false-positive numbers are reported.
core_contribution: The paper proposes the 'MCP Gateway', a dedicated intermediary that externalizes security from self-hosted Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers for enterprise AI integration. The author claims three contributions: (a) a reference architecture for MCP Gateways validated through a proof-of-concept implementation; (b) threat-model mappings with corresponding mitigation strategies; and (c) tool-specific implementation guidelines. The central design idea is a 'separation of concerns' introduced by the 2025-03-26 MCP specification's OAuth 2.1 support: the Gateway takes on authorization responsibilities (OAuth 2.1 flows, token validation, enterprise identity integration) while backend MCP servers focus only on tool/resource provision. The Gateway's five components are: a Security Proxy (TLS termination, rate limiting, forward-auth delegation), an Authentication Gateway (OAuth 2.1 + IdP integration), Zero-Trust Tunnelling (identity-aware encrypted tunnels isolating backends), Security Middleware (deep inspection, threat detection, centralized logging), and the simplified Backend MCP Servers. This is the paper's own proposal, distinct from the cited Narajala & Habler framework and the cited MAESTRO framework, which the author uses only as external evaluation lenses.
threat_model: Enterprise self-hosted MCP deployments where developers must otherwise handle OAuth, API security, and threat mitigation themselves. The Gateway aims to reduce attack surface by isolating sensitive auth logic and preventing direct public-internet exposure of MCP servers. Threats are enumerated via the MAESTRO 7-layer framework (Table I): Security Proxy defends against Denial of Service and Protocol Abuse (Layer 4); Auth Gateway against Agent Identity Attack, Lack of Auditability, Regulatory Non-Compliance (Layers 3,6); Zero-Trust Tunneling against Data Exfiltration and Side-Channel/Information Leakage (Layers 4,6); Security Middleware against Tool Poisoning and Threat Detection Failure (Layer 5); Backend MCP Servers against Injection Attacks, Framework Evasion, and Host System Compromise (Layers 3,4). A second qualitative evaluation maps the architecture against the security requirements of the cited Narajala & Habler framework (identity/access subversion, network segmentation, resource exhaustion, tool misuse, injection).
limitations: The author's own stated limitations: complexity of integrating components; performance overhead from security processing; key/token management burden; difficulty tuning threat-detection rules; and reliable tunnel management. A specific maturity caveat is raised: Pangolin, which underpins the tunnelling/management framework, was less than six months old at time of writing and depends on WireGuard and other underlying tech, so its security resilience is unproven. Beyond the author's list, the strongest limitation is evidential: validation is a single-instance qualitative PoC by the author with no adversarial testing, no quantitative metrics, no comparison benchmark, and the gateway software (MCPAuth) is the author's own. The comparison with alternatives (standard API gateways, public MCP gateway solutions) is asserted qualitatively rather than measured.

quantitative_results: No quantitative performance or security metrics reported; evaluation is explicitly qualitative.

PoC component versions (Table II, for reproducibility): Traefik V3.3.3; WireGuard 2023-12-11 release; CrowdSec V1.6.8; CrowdSec Bouncer Traefik plugin V1.4.2; Pangolin (incl. Gerbil/Newt/Badger) V1.2.0; Docker V28.1.1; MCP Inspector V0.10.2.
Deployment OS: Ubuntu 22.04 64-bit on a single public-facing VPS.
Unauthenticated requests receive an HTTP 401 to trigger .well-known/oauth-authorization-server metadata discovery.
baselines_compared: Standard API Gateways (qualitatively; said to lack MCP-specific threat understanding)
Public MCP gateway solutions (qualitatively; said not to fully address enterprise self-hosted needs)
